massive.

Outcomes and outputs rather than tools.

Hey.

How do you go about researching a topic?
How do you go about creating an outline?
How do you go about executing against that outline?
How do you style and edit a piece of content?
How do you cross-link?
All the different jobs to be done, if you will, of creating amazing content.

We started to break it down into workflows because what we want to do is take the ultimate expert in all of these and think about their thought patterns, adapt it a bit for the companies we're delivering it to and running it, right?
And then with your guidance along the way, with our guidance along the way.
But what we started to see was that none of that scale.
And part of that was because a lot of these tools out there that are meant to help you build these workflows with AI, they're really prototyping tools.
And that was like kind of the biggest shift that we had in our mentality and kind of how we approached it was our CTO co-founder, Daniel, kind of how he was approaching this problem was saying, hey.
We need to go back to code because code is what allows us to run this at scale and learn from it, right?

And I think that's the biggest thing to kind of remember as you go through and as you're working here is like the reason we're delivering services is to understand the patterns of what it takes to deliver a great work product or a great output, right?

So think of it as you go into ChatGPT and you copy and paste a bunch of things and you do a bunch of things and then like you have really good thought processes and you come out with something amazing at the end.
That's not reproducible and you can't learn from that, right?
Now you compare that to what developers and programmers are experiencing today with things like Cursor and others.

The reason those coding agents are getting better and better by the day is because it's learning from every diff and they're there and they're changing things and they're writing and you can learn from that.
All of it.
And you can figure out what context to include in real time.
And you can just accelerate that their productivity so much.

And so a lot of what we're building is not just delivering the services, although it's really, really critical, but we're delivering the services in order to figure out what are the patterns of great work so that we can learn from those patterns and those activities, right?
And in the process of doing that, we need to have tools.
Hey, there's this API, perplexity API to do research.
Hey, there's this other API.
to scrape pages.

It's doing so many different processes in the background.

To deliver the services we're delivering.

That's, you
You know, that's already more than it would require probably a hundred times more people here in order to deliver the same outputs, right?

Again, if the jobs should be done that you're performing today, next week is the exact same and take the same amount of time and you've learned nothing from it, and then a month from now and then six months from now stay the same, we're doing something wrong, right?

And so instead, first principles is the most important thing, which is you want to approach, let's say you are a content manager and you're reviewing a piece of content from a workflow that generated this, right?
It might be that the first time around, you're looking at it and you're thinking,
Critically, and you know the context because you study the company, you're supporting, and you're saying, hey, this is missing this, this, and this, and this is why it's missing this.

Like, but the idea is over time, we should learn from those things.
And the more we're learning from it, then the more we're able to then, instead of spending 10.

And so that's the idea is not to just, you know, day in and day out, keep doing what we're doing.

But I always think from the first principles of if every single person in this company is building leverage for themselves, right, then as we scale as a company, every week we should be getting better and we should have more and more leverage on the work that we're doing.
And if we keep doing that, then we'll scale really efficiently and also learn from that process and help other clients as well.

Like you all, right?

And moving that process along.
So then we can hire one for every a thousand person that applies versus, you know, having to spend hundreds and hundreds of hours to try to get to people that are the best fit, right?
And so these are all things that have potential.

if you think about it, the last thing I'll say is like, the platform that we're building today that you all will be mostly like exposed to for those on the delivery side is the, think of it as the content OS.
Which is where we'll be able to do a lot of work.
What I showed you previously is the AI workflow engine, which is the runtime and also like a lot of the
Workflows that are running this, and what we're building now is the Learn Engine, and then after that, the Task Hub.
So think of the Task Hub as a way to figure out, like, learn from what you're best at, so that we can assign the best tasks that are best for you, right?
And then also to be able to tap into external expertise.

We've been.

It's about understanding your.
audience.

How do make this experience better?

There's all these aspects.

They just want one point of contact that will take all of it on and do all those things.
Oh, this thing is consuming a bunch of time.
Don't worry, I got it, right?
Okay.

And more of that on, and the ones that we haven't done well with customers, we kind of left them with the bottlenecks and the headaches, right?
So there's some accounts where we could have been doing a lot of the publishing ourselves, but instead we're saving us draft and letting them figure it out.
And then they don't publish, or they're bottleneck, or they're too busy, and then six months in, they don't see the results because we're leaving it up to them.
And so the more we can, you know, take it on ourselves so that we're not blocked and we can drive the right results, the better.
Any questions?
I'm interested to know, just one more question.
So right now, like in our roles, for example, one of the instructions we have is like delete processes itself.
And so I'm interested to know, is there like a feedback loop of like if there are signals, if the content is performing well, and how does that tie into improving?
The workflows that we have right now.
Yeah.

So right now there are, I think, one project that Kirkland on the EPD team has been working on, on ingesting data from Google Search Console and even like web analytics from our customers to understand like how it's doing.

But regardless of that, I think it's really important to, the way I kind of think about it is every single week that we're publishing for customers, let's say we're doing five pieces of content a week, either refreshing or new, that's five opportunities to learn, right?
And so early on, the way, when I was managing accounts and doing it myself, the way I would think about it is like, hey, we're putting, we're planting five seeds.
What are we learning from these five seeds next week, right?
And what are we doing differently next week?
so if every week, like I said, we're doing things the exact same way, we didn't learn from the last cycle, and we're not applying any of those learnings, then.
And over time, that's when things don't go well.
So for instance, like we, one of the customers that we lost, attention.com, that's what we did.
We essentially just blindly published 200, not blindly, but we published 200 articles over six months and they saw almost no growth, right?
Well, that's a bad example of what we don't want to do.
What we want to do is like say, hey, we're trying this strategy a week, two weeks.
What are we seeing?
Are those pages being indexed?
Are they starting to rank for certain keywords?
Are they starting to get clicks?
Why not?
Hey, why are they getting so many impressions and no clicks?
What can we do about it, right?
Hey, are these the right topics?
Are these not the right topics?
Is the audience clicking on the page and then immediately bouncing?
Why is that?
Is it because of the way the page is set up or is it because of the content?
Is it because of the layout?

Is like all those critical thinkings of kind of asking those questions so that if we improve, help our customers improve by 1% every week is actually way better than if we're, you know, trying to do big, big improvements, right?
And so,
So that's kind of the mentality that I think is important.

And then over time, we'll try to ingest a lot of that data more and more to be able to like more programmatically say, hey, here's a signal here that you need to pay attention to do something about it as well.
Okay.
And are we like, if there are like constant Google updates, especially in the past months, are we also like responding to that and how we update the workflows?
Can you say that again?
Which updates on the workflow?
Like, for example, yeah, like Google content updates or like they're flagging this and that.
Yeah, Dave on the team, one of our directors started a channel on Google updates as well.
And I think like we're paying attention as much as we can.
And I think within accounts is important.
I should look and see, like, hey, are the latest, like, Google updates having a meaningful impact for this customer?
Why, why not?
What can we do to respond to it?

You know, and I think there's always a balance of how do you pay attention to these big shifts, obviously, and responding as quickly as possible, especially if it's having a negative impact on any customers.
But at the same time, the more we can optimize for the audience, the humans, and creating content is extremely useful to the humans.
And what these algorithms and platforms and tools are trying to do is just become the best answer for those humans, right?
And so great content is going to be great content.
And if it's highly useful for the audience we're trying to go after, I always believe that eventually we'll get rewarded, you know?

And so if it's, like, really well written, really well organized, really well positioned, well researched, well cited, and it really hits on the right voice that resonates with the audience, and you do a lot of the hygiene thing.
I think eventually it gets rewarded one way or another.
Yeah.
Yeah, I think so.
The helpful content that they really are looking for.
Awesome.
Well, any last questions before we wrap up?

Because I'm working on the assignment.
We're all working on the assignment.
Hello.

Hi, Marisol.

Yeah.
Yeah.

Or does the managing editor have a say about that since they're the ones talking to the clients more?
What I got from our pod director.
Yeah, that's a great question.
So the way to think about the directors versus managing editors versus content managers is so directors are helping.
Okay.
Set the overall strategy and connect with stakeholders, right?
So I was a CMO and overall, like what I wanted to hear was like someone that has similar experiences as me, right?
That has been a leader, that has helped other brands grow.
That's the person I want to kind of interact with more initially on the strategy, right?
And setting the right strategy.
And then the managing editors kind of thinking through that strategy and understanding the customer deeply as well.
And, but then setting the bar of what amazing content should look like, right?
And then content managers are executing against those assignments and making sure you are delivering above that bar, essentially, right?
Of what amazing content should look like on an individual basis, right?
Then over time, the more experience you have and the more you're familiar with customers and the more you're working on the content, then hopefully like you're finding opportunities.
Maybe you're creating.

You know, a topic or an assignment, and then within that, there's all these other topics that come across, and you're learning about them, and you're like, oh, this would be actually a great assignment, right?
And then you're working with your ME to say, hey, like, these assignments are coming up, or hey, I'm noticing this and that, and you're surfacing that.
And then over time, you know, then you can start to really guide the strategy more and pay attention to the signals.

And then as you're supporting, whether it's through weekly updates, or looking at the numbers, and however you're working with your managing editor, there's a ton of opportunity to take more and more ownership, right?

And so the goal is for everyone to continue to grow and take more ownership, but starting from the place of like, hey, the more you can focus on making sure what we're delivering to customers exceeds the bar of expectation, right?
And the more the MEs are setting that bar, and then executing against the strategy, and the more directors can spend the time building the relationship.
Relationship with the executives and and defining kind of like the higher strategy.

OK, thank you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Onboarding session to discuss GrowthX's strategy, vision, and operational approach for new team members.](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=4.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX aims to enhance human expertise with AI, not replace it, by turning knowledge into reproducible patterns](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)
  - [The company focuses on delivering comprehensive content services rather than just tools, targeting a potential $3.8B market](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=548.0)
  - [GrowthX is building a scalable AI workflow engine to learn from and improve content creation processes](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=698.0)
  - [The goal is continuous improvement, with team members expected to increase efficiency and add value over time](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=926.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Unique Approach

  - [Focus on augmenting human expertise with AI, not replacing humans](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)
  - [Deliver comprehensive content services rather than just tools or one-off services](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=470.0)
  - [Take on entire "lanes" of work for clients to provide more value and learn from the process](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=484.0)
  - [Target market of companies already paying for content tools but not getting desired outputs](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=548.0)

### Scalability and Workflow Development

  - [Broke down content creation into specific tasks (planning, research, outlining, etc.)](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=650.0)
  - [Developed AI workflows to replicate expert thought patterns for each task](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=650.0)
  - [Shifted from prototyping tools to custom code for better scalability and learning](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=698.0)
  - [Running hundreds of thousands of workflow instances monthly (e.g., 446,000 deep researcher workflows in the last month)](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=886.0)

### Continuous Improvement Philosophy

  - [Team members expected to critically analyze their work and find efficiencies](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=926.0)
  - [Goal is to reduce time spent on repetitive tasks and increase value-added activities](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=926.0)
  - [Feedback loop between human experts and AI workflows for mutual improvement](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=908.0)
  - [Emphasis on learning from each content piece published (e.g., "planting seeds" analogy)](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1520.0)

### Platform Development

  - [Building a "Content OS" for efficient work and learning](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1187.0)
  - [Developing an AI workflow engine as the core runtime](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1192.0)
  - [Creating a Learn Engine to capture insights from human interventions](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1255.0)
  - [Planning a Task Hub for optimal task assignment and external expert integration](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1208.0)

### Future Plans and Expansion

  - [Potential to monetize APIs or create micro-apps from existing workflows](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1101.0)
  - [Exploring applications in other domains (e.g., using AI for hiring processes)](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1132.0)
  - [Aiming to ingest performance data (e.g., Google Search Console) to inform strategy](https://fathom.video/share/h4bhybEzu8JMaEg211WJgUL7b__YS8-U?tab=summary&timestamp=1652.0)

## Next Steps
